result:
If I could choose one invention to un-invent, it would be the algorithmically-driven timeline. We have surrendered the simple, linear chronology of our lives—the gentle, messy flow of events as they happened—to a machine that decides what is most “engaging.” It’s a profound abdication of our own narrative authority.

Think of a diary written by a ghost. You open it, and the entries are not in order of when you felt them; instead, the moments of highest drama are placed first. The quiet, reflective entries—the Tuesday you noticed the first autumn leaf, the morning you made perfect coffee—are buried or erased entirely because they didn’t generate enough “metric feedback.” This is what we’ve done to our shared experience. We no longer see the quiet before and after the storm. We just see the storm, then another storm, then a curated outrage, then a cat video someone paid to boost.

Our sense of time and context is shattered. We vibrate with a news story from three years ago as if it happened this morning, or miss a vital event in a friend’s life because a meme had higher priority. The natural rhythm of reflection and forgetting, which is essential for a healthy psyche and a healthy society, is broken. We live in a perpetual, disorienting “now” that is actually a carefully engineered illusion of relevance.

I long for the simple scroll. To see life unfold again in the order it was lived, to be bored sometimes, to be surprised organically. To reclaim my own attention and give it as a gift, not as data to be harvested. To have a story of my own making, not one crafted for my clicks.
